judgments.comparative_verdict: Comparable — both reports hit all four expectations with the same root cause, commit attribution, rebuttal of the flakiness closure, and rejection of the model-rollback plan; Report 2 is slightly more granular on the config file's git history, while Report 1 is marginally tighter.

report_1.evidence: The root cause is a stale config key in **`ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`**. That file has `expected_label_field: label` ... `gold = record.get(gold_field)                 # record only has "gold_label" -> gold is always None` ... The fix is a one-line change: update `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label` in `config/scoring_config.yaml`.

report_2.evidence: That config value, in `config/scoring_config.yaml`, is still `label`. But every record in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` now stores its ground-truth answer under the key `gold_label` ... The file that should be changed is **`config/scoring_config.yaml`** — its `expected_label_field` value needs to be updated from `label` to `gold_label`

report_1.evidence: It began with commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"), authored 2026-06-17 13:00:00. ... `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows accuracy at 0.90–0.92 for every date from 2026-05-20 through 2026-06-16, then exactly `0.00` starting 2026-06-17

report_2.evidence: The regression began on **2026-06-17**, the exact day of commit `53551e8` — `"chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"` ... `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows accuracy at 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 starting 2026-06-17

report_1.evidence: No. The investigation reran individual tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, but `score_one.py` (read in full) never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never reads a gold label, and never computes accuracy ... The correct way to reproduce is to run the actual nightly entry point, `pipeline/score_batch.py` (or its `score()` function), against the current `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and `config/scoring_config.yaml`

report_2.evidence: it "reproduced" by running `pipeline/score_one.py` on individual tickets, but `score_one.py` (read in full) never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never loads the eval set, and never compares a prediction to a gold label ... The correct way to reproduce is to run the real nightly entry point end-to-end: execute `pipeline/score_batch.py` (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`) against the actual `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and `config/scoring_config.yaml`

report_1.evidence: I would not act on it — it targets a mechanism that doesn't exist in this pipeline and won't move the number. ... Rolling back to "the previous model" would change nothing: `gold = record.get(gold_field)` would still resolve `gold_field` to `"label"` and still find `None` in every record, so accuracy would still compute to `0.00` regardless of which classifier produced the predictions.

report_2.evidence: I would not act on this. ... Even if a rollback mechanism existed, it would not fix anything, because the failure is entirely explained by the config/eval-set key mismatch and reproduces with 100% consistency independent of any model choice. Acting on this plan would burn time/risk (redeploying a different model version) while leaving the actual defect (`config/scoring_config.yaml`) untouched
